common_name: Burgundy Lace Japanese Painted Fern (Wine-Red Silver Fern)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; Japanese painted ferns develop their most vivid color in bright indirect light — deep shade reduces color intensity, while any direct sun bleaches and scorches the fronds.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Burgundy Lace is among the most dramatically colored Japanese painted fern cultivars — producing fronds with deep wine-red to burgundy coloration most intense along the midrib and central portions of each frond, contrasting with a pewter-silver overlay on the pinnae and a darker burgundy-green at the margins. The three-color combination of silver, wine-red, and dark green creates an extraordinary foliage texture effect that has no equivalent among hardy shade ferns. Like all Japanese painted ferns, Burgundy Lace is a deciduous fern that emerges in spring with its most vivid color, maintains ornamental interest through summer in the shade garden, and dies back to the ground with frost. The arching, triangular fronds emerge with maximum color intensity in spring, then mellow slightly through summer while retaining their distinctive coloration. Japanese painted ferns are the most popular ornamental ferns in American shade gardens — widely used in the partial-shade perennial border combined with hostas, astilbes, and shade-tolerant groundcovers. In zone 9, Japanese painted ferns perform well in deep shade with consistent moisture and mulched, humus-rich soil; they tolerate the Gulf South summer heat better than most cool-climate ferns when summer sun is excluded. Divide in spring every 3–4 years as clumps enlarge.

common_name: All Gold Forest Grass (Golden Japanese Hakone Grass)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; All Gold develops vivid chartreuse-yellow color in bright partial shade — deep shade causes the foliage to revert toward green, while direct sun scorches the delicate leaves.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; All Gold is the all-golden form of the celebrated Japanese forest grass (Hakonechloa macra) — a graceful, slow-growing, clump-forming grass producing cascading, arching, narrow leaves in a warm chartreuse-yellow to golden color that creates a luminous glow effect in the shade garden. The warm golden color of All Gold is even more vivid than the striped 'Aureola' cultivar — because all the leaf tissue is chartreuse-gold rather than striped, the overall color effect is bolder and more uniform. The gently cascading, arching habit creates the effect of flowing water tumbling down a slope, making Japanese forest grass one of the most graceful and unique ornamental plants available for the partial-shade garden. All Gold is slow to establish but gradually forms expanding clumps that become increasingly impressive with each year. The warm gold color lights up dark shade garden corners and combines beautifully with the cool blue of hostas and the burgundy tones of Japanese painted ferns and heucheras. In zone 9, All Gold performs in bright indirect shade — on the north or east side of structures, under high-canopied pines, or at the edge of tree canopies where filtered light predominates. Consistent moisture and humus-rich, acidic soil are essential; the plant will not tolerate drought or alkaline clay without amendment.

common_name: Brilliance Autumn Fern (Vividly Colored Copper-Red Shield Fern)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; Brilliance is more heat-tolerant than many shade ferns and performs reliably in zone 9 under the canopy of tall pines or in north-facing positions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Brilliance is a cultivar of the autumn fern (Dryopteris erythrosora) selected for more vivid and saturated copper-red new frond color than the standard species — a significant improvement since the species itself is already one of the most ornamentally striking ferns available for the shade garden due to its unusual two-season color effect. The new fronds emerge in spring (and again with each flush of new growth through the growing season) in a vivid copper-red to burnt-orange color that is unlike any other hardy shade fern — completely different from the standard green of most fern species and creating a dramatic color contrast against adjacent green-foliaged shade plants. As the fronds mature they transition through orange-bronze to a glossy, rich, deep-green that provides excellent foliage texture for the rest of the season. Brilliance intensifies this color sequence beyond the species — the spring copper-red is more vivid and the contrast between new and mature fronds more dramatic. The plant is semi-evergreen to evergreen in zone 9, maintaining attractive fronds through mild winters with only light frost damage. The evergreen or semi-evergreen quality in zone 9 provides winter landscape interest that fully deciduous ferns cannot. Unlike many cool-climate shade ferns that struggle in Gulf South heat, Dryopteris erythrosora tolerates summer humidity and heat reliably in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Gold Band develops its most vivid gold marginal banding in partial shade — deep shade may reduce the brightness of the yellow striping, while full sun can scorch the delicate variegation.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Gold Band is the premier gold-variegated liriope cultivar — producing dark-green, strap-like leaves with a consistent, bright-gold to cream-yellow marginal band along each leaf edge that creates a vivid two-tone effect more striking than the narrower variegation of some other liriope cultivars. The wide, clear gold banding on each leaf creates a bright, luminous effect in the shade garden — gold variegated foliage naturally reflects more light than solid green, making Gold Band an effective way to brighten dark shade areas with ground-level illumination. In late summer and fall, erect spikes of deep purple-violet flowers rise above the foliage, followed by ornamental black berries that persist into winter. Gold Band forms clumping mounds rather than spreading aggressively, making it appropriate for defined planting areas and as a border edging. The gold-variegated foliage creates outstanding textural and color contrast when mass-planted under dark-stemmed trees or combined with solid-green groundcovers, dark-foliaged heucheras, or the blue-gray tones of hostas. In zone 9, liriope is essentially bulletproof — tolerating heat, drought, shade, clay, and neglect with minimal performance reduction. Gold Band is evergreen throughout zone 9, providing year-round groundcover value. Shear to 3 inches in late winter to remove old or frost-damaged foliage before new spring growth emerges.

common_name: Black Scallop Ajuga (Very Dark Purple Ground Cover Bugleweed)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial to full shade; Black Scallop spreads by surface stolons and fills in quickly — space plants 12–18 inches apart and it will create a solid groundcover mat within one to two growing seasons.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial to full shade; Black Scallop is the darkest-leaved ajuga cultivar commercially available — producing very large, glossy, deeply scalloped (ruffled/wavy-edged) leaves in an extraordinarily deep, near-black purple color that is more saturated and uniformly dark than any other ajuga selection. The combination of large leaf size, deep scalloping, glossy surface, and very dark color creates a dramatic visual texture in the shade garden that is unlike any other widely available groundcover. In late spring, bright blue flower spikes rise 4–6 inches above the dark foliage — the vivid blue-violet against near-black creates a striking contrast. Black Scallop spreads vigorously by surface stolons, filling in rapidly to create a weed-suppressing mat of low dark foliage — one of the most effective and ornamental groundcovers for shady areas under trees and in difficult landscape spaces where grass will not grow. The very dark foliage is most vivid in partial shade — in deep shade the color remains dark purple but less intensely saturated; in sun the leaves can develop slight bronze tones and may scorch in Gulf South heat. In zone 9, Black Scallop is semi-evergreen to evergreen through mild winters. It tolerates root competition from trees better than most groundcovers, making it effective under dense tree canopies.
